issue: Researcher/dossiers/ai_agents.md passes at 0.80 — high source quality, intact cross-source agreement, current freshness, complete handoffs. Stage discipline drags the score: three claims (clm-2026-06-02-004, 005, 006) carry weight >= 0.7 with verified=false in claims.jsonl, yet the dossier presents them as established fact. Underlying primary sources exist; verification just has not been recorded.
narrative: The dossier is real, dense, and useful. The gap is internal: claims with primary-source backing have not been promoted from verified=false to verified=true in the claims ledger, and the dossier prose does not flag the difference. Recommend a focused verification sweep: clm-2026-06-02-004 (LangChain 1.0 standard — src-003, src-006, src-015 all primary), clm-2026-06-02-005 (persistent memory frontier — src-017, src-018 both primary arXiv), clm-2026-06-02-006 (memory security class — src-018 primary arXiv). All three could pass verification against existing sources. Until then, dossier prose should mark these as 'high-weight, unverified' rather than asserting as fact.

issue: Claim clm-2026-06-02-007 (Artemis II flew Apr 1, 2026) is asserted at weight 0.99 from a single secondary source (Researcher synthesis) with zero primary sources captured in the trail; the synthesis report itself acknowledges primary sources are cited-but-not-captured (NASA press release for Artemis II success is the obvious target). Audit policy hard rule violated: weight >= 0.6 requires >= 1 primary source in trail. Underlying event is high-confidence (publicly announced and historical), but dossier-grade promotion needs the primary capture.
narrative: What was checked: source record src-2026-06-03-001 (type=secondary, trust=0.7, self-referential URL, honest trust_notes); finding fnd-2026-06-03-001 (single source_id, claim collapses 4 separate factual claims); claim clm-2026-06-02-007 (weight 0.99, verified=false, supporting_findings=[fnd-2026-06-03-001]); report reports/artemis_status_mid2026.md (11 lines, no inline primary citations); dossier dossiers/artemis_program.md (template consistent with sibling dossiers but contains 2 factual errors: line 20 references fnd-artemis-2026-06-03-001 which is not the actual finding ID, and claims the finding is not in findings.jsonl when it is). Cross-source agreement is partial: the synthesis references multiple primaries internally but they are not independently captured. Stage discipline: raw capture missing (no raw/aerospace/ folder); no run receipt for the 2026-06-03 artemis pass; 'aerospace' lane is not in the Researcher's documented source plan. Freshness: data is fresh (fetched 2026-06-03T20:30) but the cadenced window for the lane is undefined. Handoff hygiene: dossier correctly routes to Verifier audit lane, no premature promotion. Verdict: NEEDS-MORE-EVIDENCE — the gap is named (missing primary source), the audit policy hard rule is cited, and the recommended action is concrete (capture NASA Artemis II success press release as a new primary source).

issue: Claim clm-2026-06-02-008 (Artemis III restructured May 13, 2026 from lunar landing to Earth-orbit docking test, late 2027; landing pushed to Artemis IV 2028) is asserted at weight 0.98 from the same single secondary synthesis with zero primary captures. Same policy violation as clm-2026-06-02-007: weight >= 0.6 requires >= 1 primary source. Claim contains both a verifiable historical event (May 13 restructure announcement) and a forward-looking target (late 2027 docking test); the restructure is the verifiable part and the obvious primary target is NASA's own May 13 announcement.
narrative: What was checked: same trail as 007 (identical source, finding, and report). The claim is structurally more complex (announcement + restructure + new target + knock-on to Artemis IV), which is a second-order concern: the underlying finding collapses four related but distinct facts (Artemis II success + Artemis III restructure + Artemis IV push + HLS progress) into a single finding, then the claim layer carves out one slice. The dossier itself flags the most important re-verification question: 'Are the late-2027 orbital test and 2028 landing dates firm, or aspirational? Apollo-era schedule history suggests the latter.' That is the right question; the answer requires the NASA primary source. Verdict: NEEDS-MORE-EVIDENCE — same gap, same action, same score. If 008 is later split into 'restructure announced' (verifiable) and 'late 2027 target' (forward-looking) components, the verifiable half should re-audit cleanly once the primary source is captured.

issue: Claim clm-2026-06-02-009 (HLS providers racing 2027 deadline; SpaceX Starship test late May 2026; propellant transfer demos later 2026; Blue Origin Blue Moon on parallel track) is asserted at weight 0.85 — already 0.13 below 007/008 — but aggregates three different factual claims (SpaceX test, SpaceX propellant transfer demo, Blue Origin Blue Moon status) into one claim, and the underlying single source is a synthesis with no primary captures. The dossier's own 'Contradictions and open questions' section flags Blue Moon: 'Blue Moon status is described at the same level as Starship HLS, but the public cadence is much quieter. Is Blue Moon on a parallel critical path or a backup?' That open question is not resolved by the trail.
narrative: What was checked: same source trail as 007/008. The claim is the loosest of the three (per the parent's prior observation): the SpaceX Starship test claim is the most concrete (specific event, late May 2026, presumably SpaceX press release / NASASpaceflight coverage available); the propellant transfer demo is forward-looking ('later in 2026' is vague); the Blue Origin Blue Moon 'parallel track' assertion is the weakest — the dossier itself is not sure Blue Moon is on a parallel critical path vs. a backup. Cross-source agreement scored lower (0.40 vs 0.50 for 007/008) because the synthesis internal mentions SpaceX more than Blue Origin. Recommend splitting the claim into 3 sub-claims (SpaceX test, SpaceX propellant demo, Blue Origin status) and capturing primary sources for each before re-adjudication. Verdict: NEEDS-MORE-EVIDENCE — same primary-source gap, plus the split_claim action to disentangle the components. Lowest score of the three (0.455) reflects the looser foundation, matching the parent session's prior observation.

issue: clm-2026-06-02-007 (Artemis II crewed lunar flyby Apr 1-10, 2026) re-audit passes at 0.88. Underlying primary source src-2026-06-03-002 (NASA Press Release 26-041) is now in the trail; supporting_findings correctly repointed from fnd-001 (synthesis) to fnd-002 (primary NASA finding). Hard rule 'weight >= 0.6 requires >= 1 primary source' satisfied (weight 0.99, primary NASA trust 0.99).
narrative: What was checked: trail clm-007 -> fnd-002 -> src-002. src-002 is a NASA primary press release (Artemis II splashdown announcement, Apr 10 2026), trust 0.99, source_lane=aerospace, type=primary. fnd-002 is a single-fact finding (Artemis II success observed) cleanly extracted. clm-007 (weight 0.99) correctly references fnd-002 in supporting_findings. Cross-source agreement scored 0.70: single primary, but the underlying event is high-confidence and publicly announced. Source quality 0.95 (not 1.0) reflects the simulation flag -- the URL and content are Andre-injected for this timeline and not yet fetch-confirmed; trust 0.99 reflects source-class authority, not fetch-confirmation. Per parent task spec, the simulation flag is metadata, not a verdict modifier, so the source still satisfies the primary-source rule. Stage discipline 0.95: chain is well-formed, single-fact finding. Freshness 1.0: event Apr 10, captured 2026-06-03, well within cadence. Process compliance 0.85: REFRESH steps for sources/findings/claims/dossier/brief all written cleanly, but raw capture (raw/aerospace/) and run receipt (runs/RUN-2026-06-03-*.md) still missing for this pass -- cross_cutting findings, not blocking. Handoff hygiene 0.85: operator-brief flags Content lane eligibility post-re-audit. Supersedes vrd-2026-06-03-002 (NEEDS-MORE-EVIDENCE). The 'red off the board' criterion is met.

issue: clm-2026-06-02-008 (Artemis III restructured May 13 2026 from lunar landing to crewed Earth-orbit docking test, targeted late 2027; lunar surface return pushed to Artemis IV 2028) re-audit passes at 0.88. Primary source src-2026-06-03-003 (NASA Media Teleconference transcript) now in trail. Weight 0.98 satisfies the primary-source hard rule.
narrative: What was checked: trail clm-008 -> fnd-003 -> src-003. src-003 is the NASA Media Teleconference transcript (May 13 2026, 2:00 PM EDT) with Administrator Nelson, AA Free, and Program Manager Watson-Morgan, trust 0.99, type=primary. fnd-003 is a single-fact finding (Artemis III restructure observed) with rich quote content. Cross-source agreement 0.70: single primary in the explicit trail, but the restructure narrative is corroborated by the SpaceX update (src-004, ref to Artemis III Earth-orbit docking test in late 2027) and Blue Origin press kit (src-005, ref to either-provider selection pending for the same mission) -- the corroboration is in different findings/sources rather than stacked on this claim, so the score does not push higher. Source quality 0.95 (simulation flag as for 007). The forward-looking schedule parts (late 2027 docking test, 2028 Artemis IV landing) inherit the primary's authority but remain schedule claims; the dossier's open question 'Are the late-2027 orbital test and 2028 landing dates firm, or aspirational?' is correctly preserved. Supersedes vrd-2026-06-03-003.

issue: clm-2026-06-02-010 (SpaceX Starship Flight 7 / IFT-7 May 27 2026, Super Heavy booster catch, orbital insertion, controlled reentry, soft splashdown; 11 of 14 HLS critical-path milestones qualified) passes at 0.85. New claim, split from deprecated clm-009. Primary source src-2026-06-03-004 (SpaceX Update) in trail. Weight 0.95 satisfies the primary-source hard rule.
narrative: What was checked: trail clm-010 -> fnd-004 -> src-004. src-004 is a SpaceX update (late May 2026) on Starship HLS progress and IFT-7, trust 0.95 (vendor self-report discount), type=primary. fnd-004 is a multi-fact finding covering both the IFT-7 event and the propellant transfer demo schedule. The 1-to-many structure (one finding supporting both clm-010 and clm-011) is acknowledged by the parent task spec as acceptable for legitimate granularity. Source quality 0.90: SpaceX primary, trust 0.95, -0.05 for vendor self-report, -0.05 for simulation flag. Cross-source agreement 0.70: single primary in trail; the IFT-7 event is independently verifiable via NASASpaceflight/NSF coverage but those corroborations are not in the trail. Stage discipline 0.85: claim cleanly carves one slice (IFT-7 event + 11/14 milestones) out of the multi-fact finding. Freshness 1.0. Process compliance 0.85. Handoff hygiene 0.85. New claim; this is its first verdict.

issue: clm-2026-06-02-011 (SpaceX Starship HLS ship-to-ship propellant transfer demo targeted Q4 2026, slipped from Q3 2026 due to cryogenic coupling rework; remaining critical-path item for Starship HLS qualification for Artemis III) passes at 0.82 -- the narrowest of the 5 PASS verdicts, by 0.0175 above the 0.80 threshold. New claim, split from deprecated clm-009. Same primary source as clm-010 (src-2026-06-03-004 SpaceX) via 1-to-many finding-to-claim structure.
narrative: What was checked: trail clm-011 -> fnd-004 -> src-004. This claim is the schedule half of the IFT-7/propellant transfer story; the 1-to-many finding-to-claim structure is acknowledged by the parent as acceptable for legitimate granularity (-0.15 on stage_discipline vs 1-to-1 norm). Source quality 0.90: same SpaceX primary. Cross-source agreement 0.60: single primary, forward-looking schedule claim, no corroboration in trail -- schedule claims are inherently less verifiable than historical events, hence the lower score. The dossier's own 'Watch' lane flags this as slip-prone. Stage discipline 0.80: 1-to-many structure is a known deviation. Freshness 1.0. Process compliance 0.85. Handoff hygiene 0.85. The narrowest PASS of the 5 -- the rubric demands primary source for weight >= 0.6 (satisfied) but cross-source agreement is structurally weaker for a forward-looking schedule claim. Re-verification on next REFRESH recommended: if the demo actually flies in Q4 2026, this claim promotes to verified=true with a single primary observation; if it slips further, demote to weight 0.7 and surface the slip in the dossier. New claim; first verdict.

issue: clm-2026-06-02-012 (Blue Origin Blue Moon MK1 on a SEPARATE critical path from Starship HLS; either provider can be selected for Artemis III Earth-orbit docking test in late 2027; MK1 first orbital test NET Q2 2027, uncrewed MK1-S cargo first late 2026; BE-4 qualification complete Apr 2026; MK1 crewed variant targeting 2029 contingent on Artemis V) passes at 0.86. New claim, split from deprecated clm-009. Primary source src-2026-06-03-005 (Blue Origin press kit) in trail. Weight 0.85 satisfies the primary-source hard rule.
narrative: What was checked: trail clm-012 -> fnd-005 -> src-005. src-005 is the Blue Origin press kit (May 2026) on Blue Moon MK1 orbital test timeline, trust 0.9 (vendor self-report + slip-history discount), type=primary. fnd-005 is a single-fact finding (Blue Moon MK1 schedule + separate critical path) cleanly extracted. Source quality 0.85: Blue Origin primary with multiple discounts applied (vendor self-report, promotional tone, slip-history, simulation flag). Cross-source agreement 0.70: single primary, but the 'SEPARATE critical path' framing is corroborated by NASA's HLS program structure (src-003 mentions both SpaceX and Blue Origin as HLS providers for the same mission) and the SpaceX update (src-004 tracks only Starship HLS). Stage discipline 0.95: chain well-formed, single-fact finding. Freshness 1.0. Process compliance 0.85. Handoff hygiene 0.85. New claim; first verdict. The prior dossier's open question 'Is Blue Moon on a parallel critical path or a backup?' is now resolved: SEPARATE critical path, with selection decision pending. The schedule portions (Q2 2027, late 2026, 2029) inherit the schedule-claim caveat from clm-011 and should be re-verified on next REFRESH.
